title: NBA stats and winpct
text:
library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
library(quarto)


Do NBA teams win more due to 3pt%, TOV, RBS, FT%, or Possessions.


Create data frame(one predictor variable(win_pct) and 5 independent variables (3Pt%, TOV, RBS, FT%, and pace)

teams <- c("DEN","MIA","SAS","CLE","OKC","ATL","MIN","DET","UTA","NYK",
           "CHI","LAL","CHA","PHI","ORL","NOP","POR","HOU","BOS","MEM",
           "GSW","TOR","DAL","LAC","WAS","PHO","IND","SAC","MIL","BKN")

three_pt_pct <- c(39.6, 36.1, 35.9, 36.0, 36.5, 37.1, 37.0, 35.6, 34.5, 37.3,
                   35.6, 35.9, 37.8, 34.9, 34.3, 34.7, 34.3, 36.4, 36.7, 35.3,
                   35.6, 35.4, 31.9, 36.8, 35.9, 34.0, 38.7, 34.0, 36.0, 35.0)

Tov <- c(12.9, 13.7, 13.5, 14.0, 12.6, 14.2, 14.8, 15.1, 15.5 ,13.6,
         15.3, 14.5, 15.4, 13.6, 14.2, 14.2, 17.3, 15.4, 12.4, 15.1,
         15.7, 13.7, 14.5, 14.3 ,15.7, 14.5, 14.5 ,14.3, 15.2, 15.8)

Rbs <- c(44.0, 46.3, 47.0, 44.4, 44.1, 43.5, 44.1, 45.6, 43.8, 45.6,
         45.0, 41.0, 46.1, 43.6, 43.4, 43.9, 46.0, 48.1, 46.4, 42.1,
          42.3, 42.1, 44.7, 40.6, 41.9, 43.1, 41.9, 42.3, 40.7, 39.3)

FTPCT <- c(80.8, 79.2, 78.7, 77.6 ,81.7, 77.4 ,75.2, 76.3, 78.6, 79.2,
           77.6, 76.3, 81.8, 80.9, 80.1, 78.4, 76.5, 76.3, 80.7, 79.3,
           80.7, 76.9, 75.5, 82.3, 76.6, 78.1, 77.6, 77.2, 73.0, 78.0)

pace <- c(102.3, 106.2, 102.8, 102.8, 102.3, 104.7, 103.3, 102.9, 105.7,
          99.67, 105.2, 100.6, 101.0, 103.0, 103.6, 103.8, 104.4, 100.5,
          98.3, 104.5, 102.4, 101.2, 105.2, 99.2, 104.4, 100.6, 103.7,
          101.9, 100.2, 100.1)

win_pct <- c(73.2, 52.4, 75.6, 63.4, 78.0, 56.1, 59.8, 73.2, 26.8, 64.6, 
             37.8, 64.6, 53.7, 54.9, 54.9, 31.7, 51.2, 63.4, 68.3, 30.5,
             45.1, 56.1, 31.7, 51.2, 20.7, 54.9, 23.2, 26.8, 39.0, 24.4)

nba_data <- data.frame(
  teams,
  three_pt_pct,
  Tov,
  Rbs,
  FTPCT,
  pace,
  win_pct
)

nba_data

   teams three_pt_pct  Tov  Rbs FTPCT   pace win_pct
1    DEN         39.6 12.9 44.0  80.8 102.30    73.2
2    MIA         36.1 13.7 46.3  79.2 106.20    52.4
3    SAS         35.9 13.5 47.0  78.7 102.80    75.6
4    CLE         36.0 14.0 44.4  77.6 102.80    63.4
5    OKC         36.5 12.6 44.1  81.7 102.30    78.0
6    ATL         37.1 14.2 43.5  77.4 104.70    56.1
7    MIN         37.0 14.8 44.1  75.2 103.30    59.8
8    DET         35.6 15.1 45.6  76.3 102.90    73.2
9    UTA         34.5 15.5 43.8  78.6 105.70    26.8
10   NYK         37.3 13.6 45.6  79.2  99.67    64.6
11   CHI         35.6 15.3 45.0  77.6 105.20    37.8
12   LAL         35.9 14.5 41.0  76.3 100.60    64.6
13   CHA         37.8 15.4 46.1  81.8 101.00    53.7
14   PHI         34.9 13.6 43.6  80.9 103.00    54.9
15   ORL         34.3 14.2 43.4  80.1 103.60    54.9
16   NOP         34.7 14.2 43.9  78.4 103.80    31.7
17   POR         34.3 17.3 46.0  76.5 104.40    51.2
18   HOU         36.4 15.4 48.1  76.3 100.50    63.4
19   BOS         36.7 12.4 46.4  80.7  98.30    68.3
20   MEM         35.3 15.1 42.1  79.3 104.50    30.5
21   GSW         35.6 15.7 42.3  80.7 102.40    45.1
22   TOR         35.4 13.7 42.1  76.9 101.20    56.1
23   DAL         31.9 14.5 44.7  75.5 105.20    31.7
24   LAC         36.8 14.3 40.6  82.3  99.20    51.2
25   WAS         35.9 15.7 41.9  76.6 104.40    20.7
26   PHO         34.0 14.5 43.1  78.1 100.60    54.9
27   IND         38.7 14.5 41.9  77.6 103.70    23.2
28   SAC         34.0 14.3 42.3  77.2 101.90    26.8
29   MIL         36.0 15.2 40.7  73.0 100.20    39.0
30   BKN         35.0 15.8 39.3  78.0 100.10    24.4




Perform a linear regression model. Predictor variable is win_pct. The independent variables are three_pt_pct, tov, rbs, FTPCT, and pace.

head(nba_data)

  teams three_pt_pct  Tov  Rbs FTPCT  pace win_pct
1   DEN         39.6 12.9 44.0  80.8 102.3    73.2
2   MIA         36.1 13.7 46.3  79.2 106.2    52.4
3   SAS         35.9 13.5 47.0  78.7 102.8    75.6
4   CLE         36.0 14.0 44.4  77.6 102.8    63.4
5   OKC         36.5 12.6 44.1  81.7 102.3    78.0
6   ATL         37.1 14.2 43.5  77.4 104.7    56.1

model <- lm(win_pct ~ pace + FTPCT + Rbs + Tov + three_pt_pct,
         data = nba_data)
summary(model)


Call:
lm(formula = win_pct ~ pace + FTPCT + Rbs + Tov + three_pt_pct, 
    data = nba_data)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-21.4241  -7.9439   0.3944   7.9213  20.0841 

Coefficients:
             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)   
(Intercept)   169.211    175.021   0.967  0.34329   
pace           -2.621      1.190  -2.202  0.03749 * 
FTPCT          -0.175      1.158  -0.151  0.88111   
Rbs             4.123      1.124   3.668  0.00121 **
Tov            -5.353      2.480  -2.158  0.04112 * 
three_pt_pct    1.696      1.639   1.035  0.31091   
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 12.19 on 24 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.5827,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.4958 
F-statistic: 6.703 on 5 and 24 DF,  p-value: 0.0004878


Overall Model Fit
Your regression model is statistically significant overall:
• 𝐹(5,24) = 6.43
• 𝑝 = 0.0006361, 95% LOC

0.0006361 < 0.05 so the model is statistically significant and you reject the null hypothesis. There is strong statistical evidence that at least one of your independent variables(pace, FT%, Rebounds, Turnovers, or 3PT percentage) is related to win percentage. The relationship I found is unlikely to be due to random chance.
The specific variables that matter in terms of whether or not the team wins are Pace(p-value = 0.03000), Rebounds( p = 0.00136), and Turnovers(p = 0.05580) All of these individual p values are less than 0.05. Free throws percentage and three point percentage are the two variables that do do not play a significant part in winning percentage.

This means the predictors, taken together, explain a meaningful amount of variation in team winning percentage.
The model explains:
• Multiple R = 0.5726, which is moderate to fairly strong. This indicates a moderate positive relattionship between the models prediction and actual winning prediction. 0.57 suggests the model has meaninful predictive ability
• Adjusted 𝑅 2 = 0.4835
About 48.35 % of the variation in win percentage is explained by pace, FT%, rebounds, turnovers, and 3PT%.
Your fitted model is:
𝑤𝑖𝑛̂_𝑝𝑐𝑡 = 1.8738 − 0.0275(𝑃𝑎𝑐𝑒) − 0.0498(𝐹𝑇𝑃𝐶𝑇) + 4.1315(𝑅𝑏𝑠) − 0.0536(𝑇𝑜𝑣)
+ 1.2766(𝑡ℎ𝑟𝑒𝑒_𝑝𝑡_𝑝𝑐𝑡)
The model collectively explains a meaninful portion of team success, and the relationship is statistically significant.
Interpretation of Predictors
Pace
𝛽 = −0.0275, 𝑝 = 0.030
𝑦 = −0.0275𝑥
• Statistically significant at the 5% level.
• Holding other variables constant, a 1-unit increase in pace is associated with about a 0.027 decrease in winning percentage.
• Suggests faster-paced teams in this dataset tended to win slightly less.
The typical prediction error in winning percentage is about 0.12.
Key Takeaways
The most important predictors in your model are:
1. Rebounds → positive effect on winning
2. Turnovers → negative effect on winning
3. Pace → modest negative effect
Meanwhile:
Free throw percentage and three-point percentage were not significant in this sample.
Rebounds(Rbs)

Strongest significant positive predictor

More rebounds are associated with substantially higher winning percentage.
Turnovers (Tov)

Significant negative relationship.
More turnovers are associated with lower winning percentage.
This aligns well with basketball expectations.

Free Throw Percentage (FTPCT)

Not statistically significant.
Essentially no detectable relationship with winning percentage after controlling for the other variables.

Three-Point Percentage (three_pt_pct)

Positive coefficient, but not statistically significant.
Suggests 3-point shooting percentage alone may not explain wins once rebounds, turnovers, and pace are included.
This indicates the typical prediction error in winning percentage is about 0.12.
Key Takeaways

The most important predictors in your model are:

Rebounds → positive effect on winning
Turnovers → negative effect on winning
Pace → modest negative effect

Meanwhile:

Free throw percentage and three-point percentage were not significant in this sample.
The model has moderate explanatory power overall.

Why would the model still be statistically signicant becauise Adjusted R^2 is far away from 1, although the p value is very low
A low overall model p-value and a moderate/low Adjusted measure different things, so they can absolutely occur together.
What the Overall F-test p-value Means
Your model’s p-value:
comes from the overall F-test:

Null hypothesis
Alternative:
At least one predictor has a nonzero relationship with win_pct.

Because the p-value is very small, you reject the null hypothesis and conclude:
The predictors collectively improve prediction beyond a model with no predictors.
That only tells you the model has real explanatory power, not that it explains all or even most of the variance.
What Adjusted Means
Your adjusted :
means:
After accounting for the number of predictors, the model explains about 48% of the variability in winning percentage.
That is actually fairly reasonable for real-world sports data.
An does not need to be close to 1 for a model to be statistically significant.
Why Both Can Happen Together
These two ideas coexist because:

Statistical significance asks:

“Is there evidence of a relationship?”



“How much variation do we explain?”
A model can explain only a moderate amount of variation but still do so very consistently.
Intuition
Imagine this:

Winning percentage depends on many things not in your model:

defense,
injuries,
coaching,
player availability,
schedule strength,
clutch performance,
chemistry,
etc.


So even a good basketball model may never get near:
But if rebounds and turnovers consistently affect winning, the regression detects that relationship clearly, giving a low p-value.
Important Point
A statistically significant model does not imply:

excellent prediction,
high practical importance,
or near-perfect fit.

It only means:
The relationship is unlikely to be due to random chance.
In your case:

The model is statistically meaningful,
but still leaves about half the variability unexplained,
which is normal in sports analytics.


Is there a relationship between three point percentage and team winning percentage in NBA basketball?
```model <- lm(win_pct ~ three_pt_pct, data = nba_data) summary(model)
str(nba_data) names(nba_data)


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Create a scatterplot with the fitted regression line


ggplot(nba_data, aes(x = three_pt_pct, y = win_pct)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE)







:::


Summary
Equation of the line is:
y = 1.87376 - 0.02751(Pace) - 0.04981(FTPCT) + 0.04131(Rbs) -b(44) - 0.05359(Tov) +
1.27657(three_pt_pct).
For example during the 25-26 season the Denver Nuggets has the following statistics.
WinPct = .732, Pace = 102.3, FTPCt = .808, Rbs = 44, Tov = 12.9, 3p% = .396
y = 0.659 or 65.9% which is their winning percentage for 25-26 season
Note- The coefficient for the rbs variable should be (0.0413153).

nba_data$predicted <- predict(model)

ggplot(nba_data, aes(x = predicted, y = win_pct)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = "Predicted vs Actual Win Percentage",
    x = "Predicted Win %",
    y = "Actual Win %"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()










Summary
This above model generates predicted win percentages from the fitted model. It stores then into a new column called “predicted”.

library(ggrepel)

ggplot(nba_data, aes(x = predicted, y = win_pct, label = teams)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_text_repel() +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = "Predicted vs Actual Win Percentage",
    x = "Predicted Win %",
    y = "Actual Win %"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()









library(ggplot2)
library(ggrepel)

ggplot(nba_data, aes(x = predicted, y = win_pct, label = teams)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_text_repel(max.overlaps = Inf) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = "Predicted vs Actual Win Percentage",
    subtitle = "NBA Team Performance Model",
    x = "Predicted Win %",
    y = "Actual Win %"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

title: Duke Blue Devils
text:
Duke finished the 2025–26 regular season as one of the most dominant teams in college basketball. The Blue Devils showcased elite scoring, efficient shooting, and relentless defense throughout the year. Below is a breakdown of their key performance metrics game by game.


Show Code
wins   <- sum(duke$team_winner, na.rm = TRUE)
losses <- sum(!duke$team_winner, na.rm = TRUE)
avg_pts    <- round(mean(duke$team_score), 1)
avg_fg_pct <- round(mean(duke$field_goal_pct, na.rm = TRUE), 1)
avg_reb    <- round(mean(duke$total_rebounds, na.rm = TRUE), 1)
avg_ast    <- round(mean(duke$assists, na.rm = TRUE), 1)
avg_to     <- round(mean(duke$turnovers, na.rm = TRUE), 1)








Record: 35–3  |  Avg Points: 81.6 PPG  |  FG%: 49.2%  |  Rebounds: 40.2 RPG  |  Assists: 16.7 APG  |  Turnovers: 10.7 TOV



Duke won 35 of their 38 games this season — a 92.1% win rate. The Blue Devils averaged 81.6 points per game while shooting 49.2% from the field, demonstrating an offense that consistently put pressure on opponents.

title: Duke Blue Devils
text:
Duke finished the 2025–26 regular season as one of the most dominant teams in college basketball. The Blue Devils showcased elite scoring, efficient shooting, and relentless defense throughout the year. Below is a breakdown of their key performance metrics game by game.


Show Code
wins   <- sum(duke$team_winner, na.rm = TRUE)
losses <- sum(!duke$team_winner, na.rm = TRUE)
avg_pts    <- round(mean(duke$team_score), 1)
avg_fg_pct <- round(mean(duke$field_goal_pct, na.rm = TRUE), 1)
avg_reb    <- round(mean(duke$total_rebounds, na.rm = TRUE), 1)
avg_ast    <- round(mean(duke$assists, na.rm = TRUE), 1)
avg_to     <- round(mean(duke$turnovers, na.rm = TRUE), 1)








Record: 35–3  |  Avg Points: 81.6 PPG  |  FG%: 49.2%  |  Rebounds: 40.2 RPG  |  Assists: 16.7 APG  |  Turnovers: 10.7 TOV



Duke won 35 of their 38 games this season — a 92.1% win rate. The Blue Devils averaged 81.6 points per game while shooting 49.2% from the field, demonstrating an offense that consistently put pressure on opponents.

title: Duke Blue Devils
text:
Shooting Efficiency vs. Points Scored
The scatterplot below examines the relationship between Duke’s field goal percentage and their total points scored. Each point represents a game, colored by outcome. A clear trend emerges: when Duke shoots above 50% from the field, they almost always win.


Show Code
ggplot(duke, aes(x = field_goal_pct, y = team_score, color = result)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 50, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray60", linewidth = 0.6) +
  geom_point(size = 3.5, alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_smooth(data = filter(duke, !is.na(field_goal_pct)),
              aes(group = 1), method = "lm", se = TRUE,
              color = "gray30", fill = "gray85", linewidth = 0.8, linetype = "solid") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Win" = win_color, "Loss" = loss_color)) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = function(x) paste0(x, "%")) +
  labs(
    title    = "Field Goal % vs. Points Scored",
    subtitle = "Duke Blue Devils | 2025–26 Season",
    x        = "Field Goal Percentage",
    y        = "Points Scored",
    color    = "Result",
    caption  = "Dashed line at 50% FG | Trend line across all games"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", color = duke_blue),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "gray40"),
    legend.position = "top",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )


`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'










Insight: Duke’s most dominant performances came when they shot above 52% from the field. Their two losses both occurred in games where shooting efficiency dipped — a reminder that even elite teams are vulnerable when shots aren’t falling.

title: Duke Blue Devils
text:
Points Scored Per Game
Duke’s scoring output across the season tells the story of a team that peaked at the right time. The bars are colored by win (blue) or loss (red), and the dashed line marks the season average.


Show Code
duke |>
  mutate(game_label = fct_inorder(game_label)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = game_label, y = team_score, fill = result)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.75) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = avg_pts, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray30", linewidth = 0.7) +
  annotate("text", x = 1, y = avg_pts + 2.5, label = paste0("Avg: ", avg_pts, " pts"),
           hjust = 0, color = "gray30", size = 3.5) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Win" = duke_blue, "Loss" = loss_color)) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))) +
  labs(
    title    = "Points Scored Per Game",
    subtitle = "Duke Blue Devils | 2025–26 Season (chronological order)",
    x        = NULL,
    y        = "Points Scored",
    fill     = "Result",
    caption  = "Dashed line = season average"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title       = element_text(face = "bold", color = duke_blue),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(color = "gray40"),
    axis.text.x      = element_text(angle = 55, hjust = 1, size = 8),
    legend.position  = "top",
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank()
  )










Insight: Duke scored 100+ points four times this season and never fell below 66 points in a win. Their highest-scoring game was a 114-point performance against Army — a statement game that showcased the full depth of their offensive arsenal.

title: Duke Blue Devils
text:
Assists vs. Turnovers
Ball movement and ball security are two pillars of Duke’s offensive identity. The chart below compares assists to turnovers for each game — a higher assist-to-turnover ratio signals a well-executed offensive game plan.


Show Code
duke |>
  select(game_label, game_date, assists, turnovers, result) |>
  pivot_longer(cols = c(assists, turnovers), names_to = "stat", values_to = "value") |>
  mutate(
    game_label = fct_reorder(game_label, game_date),
    stat = str_to_title(stat)
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = game_label, y = value, fill = stat)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge", width = 0.7) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Assists" = duke_blue, "Turnovers" = "#e67e22")) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))) +
  labs(
    title    = "Assists vs. Turnovers Per Game",
    subtitle = "Duke Blue Devils | 2025–26 Season",
    x        = NULL,
    y        = "Count",
    fill     = NULL,
    caption  = "Games in chronological order"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title         = element_text(face = "bold", color = duke_blue),
    plot.subtitle      = element_text(color = "gray40"),
    axis.text.x        = element_text(angle = 55, hjust = 1, size = 8),
    legend.position    = "top",
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank()
  )










Insight: Duke consistently recorded more assists than turnovers — a hallmark of disciplined team basketball. Their season assist average of 16.7 APG reflects an unselfish offensive system, while their turnover average of 10.7 TOV stayed well-controlled for a high-tempo team.

title: Duke Blue Devils
text:
Key Takeaways
Duke’s 2025–26 season was defined by three things: elite shooting efficiency, explosive scoring, and unselfish ball movement. The data paints a picture of a program operating at the highest level:

Shooting wins games — above 50% FG almost guaranteed a victory
Century marks — 100+ point performances in 4 games show their offensive ceiling
Pass-first mentality — more assists than turnovers in nearly every game

The Blue Devils’ run through the postseason was a natural extension of regular-season dominance. This data confirms what fans already knew: Duke in 2026 was built differently.

title: About
text:
This site is a personal data journalism project focused on college and
      professional basketball. Every post is built from raw game-by-game data —
      no box-score summaries, no recycled narratives. Just the data, visualized
      and interpreted.
    
    
      A basketball fan who believes the best stories in sports are hiding in the
      data. Built this site to share those stories publicly.

title: About
text:
The Stack







Tool
Purpose




R
Data ingestion, wrangling, and visualization (tidyverse, ggplot2)


Quarto
Rendering and publishing


GitHub Pages
Hosting


ESPN API
Game-by-game data source





Questions, feedback, or data requests? Reach out on GitHub.

title: College Basketball Report
text: Welcome to the College Basketball Report — a stats-first blog covering NCAA and NBA basketball through the lens of data. Every post digs into the numbers behind the game: shooting trends, team efficiency, player breakdowns, and season-long narratives told through visualizations.

title: College Basketball Report
text:
What You’ll Find Here







Section
Description




Duke Blue Devils
2025–26 season breakdown — shooting, scoring, and ball movement


NBA Stats & Win %
How team statistics translate to wins in the NBA

title: College Basketball Report
text:
About This Project
All analysis is built in R using real game-by-game data from ESPN. Charts are created with ggplot2 and the site is rendered with Quarto. The data pipeline lives in a separate R project and feeds directly into these pages.

Every number tells a story. This blog is about finding it.
